### 5.2: Linux

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- **Notes:** ________________________________________________

### 5.3: Windows

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds (note: stdin workaround in CLI for Windows)
- [ ] Paths use correct separators throughout
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- [ ] .gitignore uses forward slashes (cross-platform compatible)
- **Known caveats to document:** Symlinks may require administrator privileges. Record any Windows-specific issues.
- **Notes:** ________________________________________________

### 5.4: Node.js Version Boundary

- [ ] Node.js 22.x: all CLI commands work
- [ ] Node.js < 22: graceful error or engines check blocks execution (verify npm/npx respects engines field)

### 5.5: Python Version Boundary

- [ ] Python 3.10: compute-score.py and all shared scripts work
- [ ] Python < 3.10: meaningful error from scripts (verify `requires-python = ">=3.10"` inline metadata works)

---

## Section 6: Documentation & Learning Material

### 6.1: Getting Started Guide Walkthrough

**Steps:**
1. Follow the Getting Started guide (docs/getting-started.md) step by step on a fresh project

**Expected:**
- [ ] Installation command works as documented
- [ ] Prerequisites table is accurate
- [ ] "First Steps" section works: SF -> QS -> BS -> CS -> SS all invoke correctly
- [ ] Common use cases section examples are valid
- [ ] Links to docs site work

### 6.2: Workflows Reference Accuracy

**Check docs/workflows.md against actual behavior:**
- [ ] All 14 workflows listed with correct trigger codes
- [ ] Step descriptions match actual step files
- [ ] Pipeline aliases documented correctly (forge, forge-quick, onboard, maintain)
- [ ] Headless mode behavior documented correctly

### 6.3: Concepts Page Accuracy

- [ ] Key terms defined correctly (forge tier, confidence tier, sidecar, etc.)
- [ ] No stale references to removed features

### 6.4: Learning Material Completeness

**Check `_skf-learn/` after installation:**
- [ ] Contains all files from docs/ directory
- [ ] No 404 page copied
- [ ] Files are readable and self-contained

### 6.5: Docs Site vs Actual Behavior

- [ ] IDE count in docs matches platform-codes.yaml count (23 IDEs)
- [ ] Tier descriptions match actual detection logic
- [ ] Pipeline alias expansions match pipeline-contracts.md

---

## Section 7: Regression & Misc

### 7.1: Legacy Target Cleanup

**Prerequisites:** An installation that previously used the old command-file approach (e.g., `.claude/commands/bmad-skf-*` files).

**Steps:**
1. Create fake legacy files: `.claude/commands/bmad-skf-test.md`
2. Run install or update

**Expected:**
- [ ] Legacy command files cleaned up during installation
- [ ] `.claude/commands/` directory removed if empty after cleanup

### 7.2: Returning User Compact Greeting

**Steps:**
1. Edit preferences.yaml: set `compact_greeting: true`
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Brief greeting (no full capabilities table)
- [ ] Asks what you'd like to work on
- [ ] Full capabilities table shown only if explicitly requested

### 7.3: Communication Language

**Steps:**
1. Edit config.yaml: set `communication_language: fr` (French)
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Ferris communicates in French
- [ ] Menu items, greetings, and workflow output in French
- [ ] document_output_language controls output file language separately

### 7.4: Custom Output Folders

**Steps:**
1. Install with custom folder names (e.g., `my-skills`, `my-forge-data`)
2. Generate a skill

**Expected:**
- [ ] Skills written to `my-skills/` (not default `skills/`)
- [ ] Forge data written to `my-forge-data/` (not default `forge-data/`)
- [ ] Status command shows the custom paths

### 7.5: Concurrent Tool Availability Changes

**Steps:**
1. Run SF, note tier (e.g., Forge)
2. Install/remove a tool (e.g., install ccc)
3. Run SF again

**Expected:**
- [ ] New tool detected on re-run
- [ ] Tier updated accordingly
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml reflects the change

### 7.6: Git Config User Name Resolution

**Steps:**
1. Verify `git config user.name` returns a value
2. Install SKF
3. Check config.yaml user_name field

**Expected:**
- [ ] user_name matches git config user.name
- [ ] If git config is unset, falls back to "Developer"

---

## Test Results Summary

| Section | Total | Pass | Fail | Blocked |
|---------|-------|------|------|---------|
| Critical Path (CP-1 to CP-7) | | | | |
| Section 1: CLI & Installer | | | | |
| Section 2: Workflow Runtime | | | | |
| Section 3: Edge Cases | | | | |
| Section 4: Output Quality | | | | |
| Section 5: Cross-Platform | | | | |
| Section 6: Documentation | | | | |
| Section 7: Regression & Misc | | | | |
| **TOTAL** | | | | |

## Issues Found

| ID | Section | Scenario | Severity | Description |
|----|---------|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | | | | |
| 2 | | | | |
| 3 | | | | |

## Sign-Off

| Role | Name | Date | Verdict |
|------|------|------|---------|
| Tester | | | PASS / FAIL |
| Reviewer | | | APPROVED / REJECTED |
```

---

### Design Rationale

**Structure:** The plan is organized with the Critical Path at the top (CP-1 through CP-7) covering the entire install-to-uninstall lifecycle including one complete workflow (QS). This is the minimum a tester must complete and takes roughly 2 hours. Remaining sections are grouped by area (CLI, Workflow Runtime, Edge Cases, Output Quality, Cross-Platform, Documentation, Regression) so a tester can work on one domain at a time.

**Why these specific critical path scenarios:** They trace the exact path every new user follows: `npx install` from registry, first agent activation and tier detection, first skill generation (QS is fastest), status check, update that preserves state, and uninstall. If any of these fail, the product cannot ship.

**Workflow coverage strategy:** Section 2 gives deep attention to the full quality path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX in scenario 2.4) and pipeline mode (2.5, 2.6) since those are the primary value flows. The remaining 10 workflows (AN, AS, US, SS, VS, RA, RS, DS, and batch/maintain pipeline) get smoke tests (2.12-2.22). This is realistic for a one-day testing window.

**What is deliberately excluded:** All behaviors already covered by automated tests (schema validation, file reference validation, Python script unit contracts, skill structural rules, workflow state consistency, markdown linting). The plan focuses purely on integration, runtime behavior, and subjective quality that only a human with an AI agent can evaluate.

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js` - Core installation logic tested by CP-1, CP-6, Section 1
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` - Agent activation, pipeline mode, and all workflow dispatch logic tested by CP-2, Section 2
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-quick-skill/SKILL.md` - Quick Skill workflow tested by CP-4, Section 2
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/platform-codes.yaml` - IDE installation targets tested by Section 1 and Section 5
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` - Pipeline aliases and data flow tested by Section 2.5, 2.6, 2.22

toolUseResult.prompt:
I need to design a comprehensive manual test plan document for bmad-module-skill-forge v1.0.0 release. The project is an npm-distributed CLI tool + agent skill module that compiles verified instruction files for AI agents.

## What automated tests already cover (DON'T duplicate these):
- Agent YAML schema validation (52 fixtures)
- Installation component integrity (file existence, step chains, 14 workflows × 53 steps)
- Knowledge base integrity (15 fragments, CSV index, cross-links)
- Workflow state field consistency (frontmatter, delta fields)
- CLI integration (fresh install, update, uninstall in temp dirs)
- Python script contracts (9 scripts, 137 test cases)
- Skill structure validation (14 rules per skill)
- File reference validation (201 refs across 163 files)
- Formatting, linting, markdown quality

## What needs manual testing (from my analysis):
### CLI & Installer
- Fresh install via npx on a real project
- Install with different IDE selections (Claude Code, Cursor, multiple IDEs, "Other" only)
- Update preserving config + sidecar state
- Uninstall completeness
- Status command accuracy
- npx execution from npm registry (not local)
- Version check notification

### Workflow Runtime (via AI agent)
- Setup (SF): tier detection with various tool combos
- Quick Skill (QS): end-to-end with real package
- Full quality path: Brief → Create → Test → Export
- Pipeline mode (forge, forge-quick aliases)
- Headless mode (-H flag)
- Health check behavior
- Batch mode (multiple briefs)
- All 14 workflows at least smoke-tested

### Edge Cases
- First-run (no sidecar, no config)
- Config missing/corrupt recovery
- Partial install recovery
- Remote vs local repo sources
- Docs-only source type
- Private repo handling
- Different tiers: Quick, Forge, Forge+, Deep
- Python script invocation from workflow steps
- Large repo performance

### Output Quality
- Generated SKILL.md quality and accuracy
- context-snippet.md correctness
- metadata.json schema compliance
- provenance-map.json citation accuracy
- Export manifest operations
- Version symlink creation

### Cross-Platform
- macOS, Linux, Windows (at minimum note Windows caveats)
- Node 22+ requirement
- Python 3.10+ and uv requirement

### Documentation & Learning Material
- Docs site accuracy vs actual behavior
- Learning material (_skf-learn/) completeness
- Getting started guide walkthrough

## Design requirements:
- The document should be a markdown checklist that a person can work through
- Group scenarios logically (by area, not by severity)
- Each scenario should have: what to do, what to expect, pass/fail checkbox
- Include prerequisites for each section
- Include a "Critical Path" section at the top for the minimum viable validation
- Keep it practical — a human tester should be able to complete the critical path in ~2 hours
- Full validation might take a day

Please design the structure and content of this document. Give me the complete outline with section headers, scenario descriptions, and expected outcomes.

content.text:
I now have a comprehensive understanding of the entire project. Let me design the manual test plan document.

Here is the complete manual test plan design for the bmad-module-skill-forge v1.0.0 release.

---

## Manual Test Plan: bmad-module-skill-forge v1.0.0

### Document Structure and Complete Content

```
# SKF v1.0.0 Manual Test Plan

> **Purpose:** Validate behaviors that automated tests cannot cover —  
> real npm registry execution, AI agent workflow runtime, cross-platform behavior,  
> output quality, and documentation accuracy.
>
> **Estimated time:** Critical Path ~2 hours | Full Validation ~8 hours
>
> **Legend:**  
> - [ ] = Not tested  
> - [x] = Pass  
> - [!] = Fail (add notes below scenario)  
> - [~] = Blocked / Skipped (add reason)

---

## Prerequisites (Global)

Before starting any section, confirm:

- [ ] Node.js >= 22 installed (`node --version`)
- [ ] Python >= 3.10 installed (`python3 --version`)
- [ ] uv installed (`uv --version`)
- [ ] A clean test directory available (not the SKF repo itself)
- [ ] Internet access for npm registry and GitHub
- [ ] An AI IDE with agent skill support available (Claude Code recommended)
- [ ] `ast-grep` installed (for Forge+ tier testing)
- [ ] `gh` CLI installed and authenticated (for Deep tier testing)
- [ ] (Optional) `ccc` (cocoindex-code) available for Forge+ testing
- [ ] (Optional) `qmd` available for Deep testing

Record your environment:

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| OS | |
| Node version | |
| Python version | |
| uv version | |
| AI IDE + version | |
| AI model used | |
| ast-grep version | |
| gh version | |
| ccc available? | |
| qmd available? | |
| Date tested | |
| SKF version tested | |

---

## CRITICAL PATH (Minimum Viable Validation)

> Complete these scenarios first. They cover the install-to-first-skill happy path
> that every user will encounter. Target: ~2 hours.

### CP-1: Fresh Install from npm Registry

**Prerequisites:** Empty test directory, no prior SKF installation.

**Steps:**
1. `cd` into the empty test directory
2. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
3. When prompted, enter a project name (e.g., "test-project")
4. Accept default output folders ("skills", "forge-data")
5. Select "Claude Code" as the IDE (or whichever IDE you are testing with)
6. Accept learning material installation

**Expected:**
- [ ] npx downloads the package from the npm registry (not a local copy)
- [ ] Banner displays with "SKF" ASCII art and version number
- [ ] All prompts render correctly with styled terminal output (no ANSI artifacts)
- [ ] Installation completes with spinner progress for each step
- [ ] `_bmad/skf/` directory created with: module.yaml, config.yaml, VERSION file, all `skf-*` skill directories, knowledge/, shared/
- [ ] `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/` directory created with forge-tier.yaml and preferences.yaml
- [ ] `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml` created and contains version, file lists, IDE list
- [ ] `.gitignore` updated (or created) with `_bmad/_memory/` entry
- [ ] IDE skill directories created (e.g., `.claude/skills/`) with all `skf-*` directories, knowledge/, shared/ copied in
- [ ] `skills/` and `forge-data/` directories created with `.gitkeep` files
- [ ] `_skf-learn/` directory created with documentation content
- [ ] Success message displays with "Get Started" instructions referencing `@Ferris SF`
- [ ] config.yaml contains correct project_name, ides list, and folder paths

### CP-2: Agent Activation and First-Run Greeting

**Prerequisites:** CP-1 completed. AI IDE open in the test directory.

**Steps:**
1. Open the project in your AI IDE
2. Invoke Ferris (e.g., type `@Ferris SF` or activate the skf-forger skill)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Ferris loads config.yaml and resolves all variables (project_name, sidecar_path, etc.)
- [ ] Ferris detects that forge-tier.yaml has `tier: ~` (null) — identifies as first-run user
- [ ] Greeting addresses you by name (from git config user.name or "Developer")
- [ ] Greeting highlights recommended starting paths: SF, QS, BS, KI
- [ ] Ferris waits for input — does NOT auto-execute any menu item

### CP-3: Setup Forge (SF) — Tier Detection

**Prerequisites:** CP-2 completed. Still in the same agent session.

**Steps:**
1. Respond with "SF" (or let it continue from CP-2 if already invoked with SF)
2. Let the autonomous workflow run to completion

**Expected:**
- [ ] Ferris loads and executes `steps-c/step-01-detect-and-tier.md`
- [ ] Each available tool is detected (ast-grep, gh, ccc, qmd) — results shown
- [ ] Tier is set correctly based on detected tools (Quick if none, Forge if ast-grep, Forge+ if ast-grep+ccc, Deep if ast-grep+gh+qmd)
- [ ] `forge-tier.yaml` is written with correct tool availability and tier
- [ ] `preferences.yaml` exists with default values (headless_mode: false, compact_greeting: false)
- [ ] Final status report displays showing the detected tier, tools, and configuration
- [ ] Health check runs — "Clean run. No workflow issues to report."

### CP-4: Quick Skill (QS) — End-to-End

**Prerequisites:** CP-3 completed. Fresh agent session recommended.

**Steps:**
1. Activate Ferris: `@Ferris QS https://github.com/chalk/chalk` (or another small, well-known package)
2. Let the workflow proceed through all 6 steps
3. When the review gate appears (step 4), review the compiled output and continue

**Expected:**
- [ ] Step 1 (Resolve Target): Repository is resolved from the URL/package name
- [ ] Step 2 (Ecosystem Check): Checks for existing skills — presents findings or proceeds
- [ ] Step 3 (Quick Extract): Extracts public API surface from the resolved source
- [ ] Step 4 (Compile): Presents compiled SKILL.md for review
- [ ] Step 5 (Validate): Validates the compiled output
- [ ] Step 6 (Write Output): Writes files to `skills/{skill-name}/v{version}/`
- [ ] SKILL.md exists and contains: frontmatter (name, version, source), Overview, API sections with function signatures, citations with file:line references
- [ ] context-snippet.md exists — summarized version suitable for passive context injection
- [ ] metadata.json exists — contains name, version, source_repo, source_type, confidence tier info
- [ ] Active version symlink or pointer set correctly (`skills/{skill-name}/active` -> `v{version}`)
- [ ] Health check runs at workflow completion

### CP-5: Status Command

**Prerequisites:** CP-4 completed (installation exists with one generated skill).

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge status` from the project directory

**Expected:**
- [ ] Shows "Skill Forge -- Status" with version
- [ ] Installation section: project name, SKF folder path, agent installed status, skill count (workflow count should show the installed workflow skills)
- [ ] IDEs section: lists the configured IDE(s) with green indicators
- [ ] Forge Tier section: shows detected tier, detection date, and tool availability for ast-grep, gh, ccc, qmd
- [ ] Output Folders section: shows skills/ and forge-data/ with existence checkmarks
- [ ] Sidecar section: shows initialized state and path

### CP-6: Update Preserving Config

**Prerequisites:** CP-4 completed. Existing installation with generated skills.

**Steps:**
1. Edit `_bmad/skf/config.yaml` — change `project_name` to "custom-name-test"
2. Note the current forge-tier.yaml contents (tier, tool detections)
3. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge update`
4. Verify preserved state

**Expected:**
- [ ] Update message displays "Quick Update" with "preserving config and sidecar"
- [ ] SKF files are replaced (VERSION file updated, workflow SKILL.md files refreshed)
- [ ] `config.yaml` still contains "custom-name-test" as project_name
- [ ] `forge-tier.yaml` still contains the previously detected tier and tools
- [ ] `preferences.yaml` is unchanged
- [ ] Manifest updated with new timestamp and version
- [ ] IDE skill directories refreshed with current files
- [ ] Generated skills in `skills/` are untouched

### CP-7: Uninstall Completeness

**Prerequisites:** CP-6 completed.

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge uninstall`
2. Review the removal plan displayed
3. Confirm when prompted

**Expected:**
- [ ] Removal plan shows categories: SKF module files, agent sidecar state, IDE skill directories, learning material, output folder scaffolding
- [ ] After confirmation: IDE skill directories cleaned (skf-*, knowledge, shared removed)
- [ ] `_skf-learn/` removed
- [ ] Output scaffolding (.gitkeep files) removed; empty skills/ and forge-data/ folders removed
- [ ] Sidecar directory removed (`_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/`)
- [ ] SKF module directory removed (`_bmad/skf/`)
- [ ] Manifest file removed (`_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml`)
- [ ] Empty `_bmad/_config/` and `_bmad/` directories cleaned up (if no other occupants)
- [ ] "SKF uninstalled successfully" message displayed
- [ ] Generated skills in `skills/` with actual content (not just .gitkeep) remain untouched — only .gitkeep scaffolding is removed

---

## Section 1: CLI & Installer (Extended)

### 1.1: Multi-IDE Installation

**Prerequisites:** Empty test directory.

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
2. Select multiple IDEs: Claude Code, Cursor, and one additional IDE
3. Complete installation

**Expected:**
- [ ] Skill directories created for each selected IDE (e.g., `.claude/skills/`, `.cursor/skills/`)
- [ ] Each IDE directory contains all `skf-*` skill directories, knowledge/, shared/
- [ ] config.yaml `ides` field lists all selected IDE codes
- [ ] Manifest `files.ide_skills` lists files for all IDE targets

### 1.2: "Other" Only IDE Selection

**Prerequisites:** Empty test directory.

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
2. Select only "Other" as the IDE
3. Complete installation

**Expected:**
- [ ] No IDE-specific skill directories created (no `.claude/skills/` etc.)
- [ ] config.yaml `ides` contains `['other']`
- [ ] Installation succeeds without errors
- [ ] Success message says "Open this folder in your IDE" (generic)

### 1.3: IDE Auto-Detection

**Prerequisites:** Test directory containing a `.claude/` directory (pre-existing).

**Steps:**
1. Create `.claude/` directory in the test project
2. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
3. Observe the IDE selection prompt

**Expected:**
- [ ] Log message shows "Auto-detected IDEs: claude-code"
- [ ] Claude Code is pre-selected in the multiselect prompt

### 1.4: Reinstall (Fresh) Over Existing Installation

**Prerequisites:** Existing SKF installation.

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
2. Select "Fresh install -- Remove everything and start over"
3. Complete with different settings (different project name, different IDE)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Warning about existing installation is displayed with version comparison
- [ ] Old installation is removed before new one is created
- [ ] New config.yaml reflects the new settings
- [ ] Previous config defaults are pre-populated in prompts (previous project name, etc.)
- [ ] Manifest reflects the fresh install action

### 1.5: Version Check Notification

**Prerequisites:** A version of the package older than the latest published version.

**Steps:**
1. Install an older version: `npx bmad-module-skill-forge@0.9.0 install` (or similar older version)
2. Run any command (e.g., `status`)
3. Observe output after command completes

**Expected:**
- [ ] After the command output, a version update notice appears
- [ ] Notice shows: "Update available: {current} -> {latest}"
- [ ] Notice includes the upgrade command: `npx bmad-module-skill-forge@latest install`

### 1.6: npx Wrapper Behavior

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge` (no subcommand)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Help output displays with available commands: install, update, status, uninstall
- [ ] Each command has a description
- [ ] Version is shown

### 1.7: Install Without Existing _bmad Directory

**Prerequisites:** Completely empty directory.

**Expected (during install):**
- [ ] Detection reports type: "fresh"
- [ ] `_bmad/` directory created from scratch
- [ ] No "existing installation" prompt shown

### 1.8: Install Into Existing _bmad Directory (Non-SKF)

**Prerequisites:** Directory with `_bmad/` but no `_bmad/skf/`.

**Steps:**
1. Create `_bmad/` directory manually (simulating BMad core)
2. Run install

**Expected:**
- [ ] Detection reports type: "bmad-ready"
- [ ] `_bmad/skf/` created alongside existing `_bmad/` contents
- [ ] No "existing installation" prompt (since `_bmad/skf/` does not exist)

### 1.9: Uninstall Without Manifest

**Prerequisites:** SKF installed, then manually delete `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml`.

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge uninstall`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Warning: "No manifest found. Reinstall first to generate one, then run uninstall again."
- [ ] No files deleted
- [ ] Clean exit

### 1.10: Cancel During Installation

**Steps:**
1. Start `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
2. Press Ctrl+C or select Cancel at the IDE selection prompt

**Expected:**
- [ ] "Installation cancelled" message displayed
- [ ] No partial files left behind
- [ ] Clean exit

### 1.11: Learning Material Opt-Out

**Steps:**
1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
2. When asked "Install learning & reference material?", select No
3. Complete installation

**Expected:**
- [ ] No `_skf-learn/` directory created
- [ ] Installation succeeds normally
- [ ] config.yaml has `install_learning: false`
- [ ] Manifest `files.learning` is empty

---

## Section 2: Workflow Runtime (Agent-Driven)

> These tests require an AI agent with skill support. Start a fresh session
> for each workflow to prevent context contamination.

### 2.1: Setup Forge — Quick Tier (No Tools)

**Prerequisites:** Installed SKF. No ast-grep, ccc, or qmd available.

**Steps:**
1. Ensure ast-grep is not on PATH (or temporarily rename it)
2. Fresh session: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Tier detected as "Quick"
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml shows: ast_grep: null/false, gh_cli: (detected or null), ccc: null, qmd: null
- [ ] tier field set to "Quick"
- [ ] Report acknowledges Quick tier positively (not dismissively)

### 2.2: Setup Forge — Forge Tier (ast-grep Only)

**Prerequisites:** ast-grep available, no ccc/qmd.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Tier detected as "Forge"
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml shows ast_grep: available/true
- [ ] tier field set to "Forge"

### 2.3: Setup Forge — Tier Override

**Prerequisites:** Completed SF at any tier.

**Steps:**
1. Edit `preferences.yaml`: set `tier_override: Quick`
2. Fresh session: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Report acknowledges the tier override
- [ ] Workflow behaves at Quick tier regardless of available tools
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml still reflects actual tool detection, but operational tier is overridden

### 2.4: Full Quality Path — Brief -> Create -> Test -> Export

**Prerequisites:** Installed SKF with tier detected. A known GitHub repository to target.

This is the most important workflow test. Run each workflow in a separate fresh session.

#### 2.4a: Brief Skill (BS)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris BS`
2. Provide: target repo URL, skill name, any scope hints
3. Review the analyzed scope
4. Confirm the brief

**Expected:**
- [ ] Step 1: Gathers intent — asks for target and skill name
- [ ] Step 2: Analyzes the target repo
- [ ] Step 3: Presents scope definition for review
- [ ] Step 4: Presents the final brief for confirmation
- [ ] Step 5: Writes `skill-brief.yaml` to `forge-data/`
- [ ] skill-brief.yaml contains: name, version, source_repo, scope, includes/excludes, language

#### 2.4b: Create Skill (CS)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris CS`
2. Provide the path to the skill-brief.yaml from 2.4a
3. Review extraction findings when prompted
4. Let compilation proceed

**Expected:**
- [ ] Step 1: Loads the brief successfully
- [ ] Step 2: Ecosystem check — reports any existing skills for this package
- [ ] Step 3: Extracts source — presents findings for review with file:line citations
- [ ] Step 5: Compiles SKILL.md
- [ ] Step 6: Validates compiled output
- [ ] Step 7: Generates all artifacts to `skills/{name}/v{version}/`
- [ ] SKILL.md has frontmatter, Overview, API sections, citations with confidence tiers
- [ ] provenance-map.json exists with entries mapping instructions to source locations
- [ ] metadata.json has correct name, version, source info
- [ ] context-snippet.md exists
- [ ] evidence-report.md exists (if applicable)

#### 2.4c: Test Skill (TS)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris TS`
2. Provide the skill name from 2.4b

**Expected:**
- [ ] Step 1: Loads the skill and its artifacts
- [ ] Step 2: Detects mode (naive or contextual depending on available source)
- [ ] Step 3: Coverage check — reports export coverage percentage
- [ ] Step 4: Coherence check (if contextual mode)
- [ ] Step 5: Score computed — shows breakdown by category
- [ ] Step 6: Report presented with PASS/FAIL verdict and score
- [ ] Report written to `forge-data/` as `test-report-{skill_name}.md`
- [ ] Python compute-score.py script is invoked and returns deterministic score

#### 2.4d: Export Skill (EX)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris EX`
2. Provide the skill name from 2.4b
3. Confirm context file updates when prompted

**Expected:**
- [ ] Step 1: Loads skill for export
- [ ] Step 2: Packages — creates or updates `.export-manifest.json`
- [ ] Step 3: Generates context snippet if not present
- [ ] Step 4: Updates platform context files (CLAUDE.md or .cursorrules or AGENTS.md) with managed section
- [ ] Step 5: Token report — shows approximate token costs
- [ ] Step 6: Summary with export details
- [ ] Managed section in context file has clear markers and references the exported skill

### 2.5: Pipeline Mode — forge Alias

**Prerequisites:** Installed SKF, tier detected.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris forge chalk` (or another target)
2. Let the entire pipeline run

**Expected:**
- [ ] Pipeline parsed: BS -> CS -> TS -> EX
- [ ] Headless mode activated automatically
- [ ] Pipeline progress: "Pipeline [1/4]: Starting BS..."
- [ ] Each workflow auto-proceeds through gates
- [ ] Data forwarded between workflows (BS produces brief, CS consumes it, etc.)
- [ ] Pipeline summary displayed at end showing all completed workflows
- [ ] If TS score below threshold (60 default), pipeline halts before EX

### 2.6: Pipeline Mode — forge-quick Alias

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris forge-quick <package-name>`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Pipeline parsed: QS -> TS -> EX
- [ ] Headless mode active
- [ ] Quick skill runs, then test, then export
- [ ] Pipeline completes or halts at circuit breaker

### 2.7: Headless Mode (-H Flag)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris QS <package-name> --headless`

**Expected:**
- [ ] All gates auto-resolve with default action
- [ ] Log messages show "headless: auto-continue" at each gate
- [ ] Progress output still displays (headless skips interaction, not reporting)
- [ ] Final output written to disk

### 2.8: Headless Mode (Preferences)

**Steps:**
1. Edit `preferences.yaml`: set `headless_mode: true`
2. Fresh session: `@Ferris QS <package-name>`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Headless mode active without passing -H flag
- [ ] Same behavior as 2.7

### 2.9: Health Check — Clean Run

**Steps:**
1. Run any workflow that completes successfully (e.g., QS)
2. Observe the health check at the end

**Expected:**
- [ ] "Health Check: Clean run. No workflow issues to report."
- [ ] No fabricated issues

### 2.10: Knowledge Index (KI)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris KI`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Displays contents of `skf-knowledge-index.csv`
- [ ] Shows 15 knowledge fragments with id, name, description, tags, tier
- [ ] Fragment paths are valid (knowledge/overview.md, etc.)

### 2.11: Workspace Status (WS)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session (after some workflows have run): `@Ferris WS`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Shows current forge tier
- [ ] Shows tool availability
- [ ] Shows active skill briefs (if any)
- [ ] Shows lifecycle position

### 2.12: Analyze Source (AN) — Smoke Test

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session in a project with actual source code
2. `@Ferris AN`
3. Provide the project path

**Expected:**
- [ ] Workflow initializes and scans the project
- [ ] Identifies skillable units
- [ ] Presents recommendations for review
- [ ] Generates skill-brief.yaml files for recommended units

### 2.13: Audit Skill (AS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A previously generated skill with accessible source.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris AS`
2. Provide the skill name

**Expected:**
- [ ] Loads the existing skill
- [ ] Re-indexes source for comparison
- [ ] Produces a drift report with severity classification
- [ ] drift-report file written to forge-data/

### 2.14: Update Skill (US) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A previously generated skill where source has changed (or simulate by modifying source).

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris US`
2. Provide the skill name

**Expected:**
- [ ] Loads existing skill and detects changes
- [ ] Re-extracts only changed exports
- [ ] Merges while preserving [MANUAL] sections
- [ ] Updated SKILL.md and provenance-map.json written

### 2.15: Stack Skill (SS) — Code Mode Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A project directory with package.json or similar manifest.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris SS`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Detects dependency manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, Cargo.toml, etc.)
- [ ] Ranks dependencies by significance
- [ ] Presents library list for confirmation
- [ ] Extracts integration patterns
- [ ] Compiles consolidated stack skill
- [ ] Outputs SKILL.md, context-snippet.md, metadata.json with skill_type: "stack"

### 2.16: Verify Stack (VS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** Architecture doc and some generated skills.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris VS`
2. Provide the architecture doc path

**Expected:**
- [ ] Cross-references skills against architecture
- [ ] Produces feasibility report (FEASIBLE / CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE / NOT FEASIBLE)
- [ ] Every verdict cites specific APIs from generated skills

### 2.17: Refine Architecture (RA) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** Architecture doc and generated skills.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris RA`
2. Provide the architecture doc path

**Expected:**
- [ ] Gap analysis performed
- [ ] Issue detection performed
- [ ] Improvements suggested with API evidence
- [ ] Refined architecture document written

### 2.18: Rename Skill (RS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** An existing generated skill.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris RS`
2. Provide old name and new name

**Expected:**
- [ ] Validation: new name does not collide with existing skill
- [ ] Copy-before-delete: new name materialized before old name removed
- [ ] All references updated (manifest, version directories, metadata)
- [ ] Platform context files rebuilt if applicable

### 2.19: Drop Skill (DS) — Soft Deprecation

**Prerequisites:** An existing generated skill.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris DS`
2. Provide skill name, select "deprecate" mode

**Expected:**
- [ ] Confirmation required before proceeding
- [ ] Manifest updated with deprecation status
- [ ] Files retained on disk
- [ ] Platform context files rebuilt to exclude deprecated version

### 2.20: Drop Skill (DS) — Hard Purge

**Prerequisites:** An existing generated skill (not the one from 2.19 if you want to preserve state).

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris DS`
2. Provide skill name, select "purge" mode

**Expected:**
- [ ] Confirmation required with warning about irreversibility
- [ ] Files deleted from disk
- [ ] Manifest updated
- [ ] Platform context files rebuilt

### 2.21: Batch Mode (CS --batch)

**Prerequisites:** Multiple skill-brief.yaml files in forge-data/.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris CS --batch`

**Expected:**
- [ ] All brief files discovered
- [ ] Each brief processed sequentially
- [ ] Separate output directories for each skill
- [ ] Summary of all compiled skills at end

### 2.22: Pipeline Mode — maintain Alias

**Prerequisites:** Existing skill with source changes.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: `@Ferris maintain`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Pipeline parsed: AS -> US -> TS -> EX
- [ ] If AS finds CLEAN drift — US skipped with "No drift detected"
- [ ] If AS finds drift — US proceeds with update
- [ ] TS and EX follow normally

---

## Section 3: Edge Cases

### 3.1: First Run — No Sidecar, No Config

**Prerequisites:** Installed SKF, but manually delete `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/`.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Ferris detects missing sidecar or forge-tier.yaml
- [ ] DOES NOT crash — presents meaningful guidance
- [ ] Suggests running SF to reinitialize

### 3.2: Missing Config File

**Prerequisites:** Installed SKF, but manually delete `_bmad/skf/config.yaml`.

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] HARD HALT: "Cannot initialize. SKF config not found."
- [ ] Suggests running skf-setup
- [ ] No crash, no stack trace exposed to user

### 3.3: Corrupt Config (Invalid YAML)

**Prerequisites:** Replace config.yaml contents with invalid YAML (e.g., `:::bad yaml:::`)

**Steps:**
1. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Meaningful error message (not a raw YAML parse stack trace)
- [ ] Actionable guidance for the user

### 3.4: Remote Repository Source (GitHub URL)

**Steps:**
1. `@Ferris QS https://github.com/lodash/lodash`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Source resolved from GitHub
- [ ] gh CLI used if available, otherwise falls back to web-accessible source
- [ ] Skill generated with correct source_repo reference

### 3.5: Package Name Resolution (npm/PyPI/crates.io)

**Steps:**
1. `@Ferris QS express` (npm)
2. (Optional) `@Ferris QS requests` (PyPI)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Package name resolved to GitHub repository via registry metadata
- [ ] Correct repository identified and used for extraction

### 3.6: Docs-Only Source Type

**Steps:**
1. `@Ferris BS` targeting a documentation URL with no source code
2. Complete the briefing process with `source_type: docs`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Workflow handles docs-only mode
- [ ] All citations are [EXT:...] format
- [ ] Test scoring applies docs-only weight redistribution (no Signature Accuracy, no Type Coverage)

### 3.7: Version-Pinned Skill

**Steps:**
1. `@Ferris QS chalk@5.0.0`

**Expected:**
- [ ] Version extracted from the invocation
- [ ] Skill generated for the specific version
- [ ] Output directory includes version: `skills/chalk/v5.0.0/`
- [ ] metadata.json records the pinned version

### 3.8: Python Script Invocation from Workflow

**Prerequisites:** Python 3.10+ and uv available.

**Steps:**
1. Run the full TS workflow on any skill (triggers compute-score.py)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Python script invoked successfully via `uv run` or direct execution
- [ ] Score returned as valid JSON
- [ ] No Python import errors or missing dependencies

### 3.9: Large Repository Performance

**Steps:**
1. `@Ferris QS https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript` (or another large repo)

**Expected:**
- [ ] Workflow does not hang or time out
- [ ] Extraction scopes appropriately (does not try to process every file)
- [ ] Result produced within reasonable time (< 10 minutes)
- [ ] Note: output quality for very large repos may be lower — document any issues

---

## Section 4: Output Quality

### 4.1: SKILL.md Structure and Content

**Prerequisites:** At least one generated skill.

**Check the generated SKILL.md:**
- [ ] Valid YAML frontmatter with: name, description (matches agentskills.io spec)
- [ ] Overview section present
- [ ] API/function sections with signatures
- [ ] Every function signature includes parameter names, types (where available), and return types
- [ ] Source citations present in format like `[AST:path/to/file.py:L42]` or `[SRC:path:L42]`
- [ ] No hallucinated content (functions that don't exist in source)
- [ ] Progressive disclosure: detailed content is in references/ or inline as appropriate
- [ ] Clean markdown formatting — no broken links, no raw HTML artifacts

### 4.2: context-snippet.md Quality

**Prerequisites:** At least one exported skill.

**Check:**
- [ ] Concise summary (not a full copy of SKILL.md)
- [ ] Contains key API entry points
- [ ] Suitable for injection into CLAUDE.md or similar context files
- [ ] Clean markdown

### 4.3: metadata.json Schema

**Prerequisites:** At least one generated skill.

**Check:**
- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Contains: name, version, source_repo, source_type
- [ ] Confidence tier information present
- [ ] Timestamps present
- [ ] Skill type field (individual or stack)

### 4.4: provenance-map.json Citation Accuracy

**Prerequisites:** A skill generated at Forge tier or above (AST citations).

**Check:**
- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Each entry maps a claim/instruction to a source location
- [ ] Source paths are relative and valid
- [ ] Line numbers reference actual code (spot-check 3-5 citations)
- [ ] Confidence tiers assigned (T1, T1-low, T2, T3)

### 4.5: Export Manifest Operations

**Prerequisites:** At least one exported skill.

**Check `skills/{name}/.export-manifest.json`:**
- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Lists exported versions
- [ ] Active version correctly identified
- [ ] Deprecated versions marked (if applicable)

### 4.6: Version Directory Structure

**Check:**
- [ ] Skills stored under `skills/{name}/v{version}/`
- [ ] Active symlink or reference points to the correct version
- [ ] Multiple versions can coexist (generate skill at two different versions)

---

## Section 5: Cross-Platform

> Run the Critical Path (or at minimum CP-1 through CP-5) on each target platform.

### 5.1: macOS

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- **Notes:** ________________________________________________

### 5.2: Linux

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- **Notes:** ________________________________________________

### 5.3: Windows

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds (note: stdin workaround in CLI for Windows)
- [ ] Paths use correct separators throughout
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- [ ] .gitignore uses forward slashes (cross-platform compatible)
- **Known caveats to document:** Symlinks may require administrator privileges. Record any Windows-specific issues.
- **Notes:** ________________________________________________

### 5.4: Node.js Version Boundary

- [ ] Node.js 22.x: all CLI commands work
- [ ] Node.js < 22: graceful error or engines check blocks execution (verify npm/npx respects engines field)

### 5.5: Python Version Boundary

- [ ] Python 3.10: compute-score.py and all shared scripts work
- [ ] Python < 3.10: meaningful error from scripts (verify `requires-python = ">=3.10"` inline metadata works)

---

## Section 6: Documentation & Learning Material

### 6.1: Getting Started Guide Walkthrough

**Steps:**
1. Follow the Getting Started guide (docs/getting-started.md) step by step on a fresh project

**Expected:**
- [ ] Installation command works as documented
- [ ] Prerequisites table is accurate
- [ ] "First Steps" section works: SF -> QS -> BS -> CS -> SS all invoke correctly
- [ ] Common use cases section examples are valid
- [ ] Links to docs site work

### 6.2: Workflows Reference Accuracy

**Check docs/workflows.md against actual behavior:**
- [ ] All 14 workflows listed with correct trigger codes
- [ ] Step descriptions match actual step files
- [ ] Pipeline aliases documented correctly (forge, forge-quick, onboard, maintain)
- [ ] Headless mode behavior documented correctly

### 6.3: Concepts Page Accuracy

- [ ] Key terms defined correctly (forge tier, confidence tier, sidecar, etc.)
- [ ] No stale references to removed features

### 6.4: Learning Material Completeness

**Check `_skf-learn/` after installation:**
- [ ] Contains all files from docs/ directory
- [ ] No 404 page copied
- [ ] Files are readable and self-contained

### 6.5: Docs Site vs Actual Behavior

- [ ] IDE count in docs matches platform-codes.yaml count (23 IDEs)
- [ ] Tier descriptions match actual detection logic
- [ ] Pipeline alias expansions match pipeline-contracts.md

---

## Section 7: Regression & Misc

### 7.1: Legacy Target Cleanup

**Prerequisites:** An installation that previously used the old command-file approach (e.g., `.claude/commands/bmad-skf-*` files).

**Steps:**
1. Create fake legacy files: `.claude/commands/bmad-skf-test.md`
2. Run install or update

**Expected:**
- [ ] Legacy command files cleaned up during installation
- [ ] `.claude/commands/` directory removed if empty after cleanup

### 7.2: Returning User Compact Greeting

**Steps:**
1. Edit preferences.yaml: set `compact_greeting: true`
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Brief greeting (no full capabilities table)
- [ ] Asks what you'd like to work on
- [ ] Full capabilities table shown only if explicitly requested

### 7.3: Communication Language

**Steps:**
1. Edit config.yaml: set `communication_language: fr` (French)
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**
- [ ] Ferris communicates in French
- [ ] Menu items, greetings, and workflow output in French
- [ ] document_output_language controls output file language separately

### 7.4: Custom Output Folders

**Steps:**
1. Install with custom folder names (e.g., `my-skills`, `my-forge-data`)
2. Generate a skill

**Expected:**
- [ ] Skills written to `my-skills/` (not default `skills/`)
- [ ] Forge data written to `my-forge-data/` (not default `forge-data/`)
- [ ] Status command shows the custom paths

### 7.5: Concurrent Tool Availability Changes

**Steps:**
1. Run SF, note tier (e.g., Forge)
2. Install/remove a tool (e.g., install ccc)
3. Run SF again

**Expected:**
- [ ] New tool detected on re-run
- [ ] Tier updated accordingly
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml reflects the change

### 7.6: Git Config User Name Resolution

**Steps:**
1. Verify `git config user.name` returns a value
2. Install SKF
3. Check config.yaml user_name field

**Expected:**
- [ ] user_name matches git config user.name
- [ ] If git config is unset, falls back to "Developer"

---

## Test Results Summary

| Section | Total | Pass | Fail | Blocked |
|---------|-------|------|------|---------|
| Critical Path (CP-1 to CP-7) | | | | |
| Section 1: CLI & Installer | | | | |
| Section 2: Workflow Runtime | | | | |
| Section 3: Edge Cases | | | | |
| Section 4: Output Quality | | | | |
| Section 5: Cross-Platform | | | | |
| Section 6: Documentation | | | | |
| Section 7: Regression & Misc | | | | |
| **TOTAL** | | | | |

## Issues Found

| ID | Section | Scenario | Severity | Description |
|----|---------|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | | | | |
| 2 | | | | |
| 3 | | | | |

## Sign-Off

| Role | Name | Date | Verdict |
|------|------|------|---------|
| Tester | | | PASS / FAIL |
| Reviewer | | | APPROVED / REJECTED |
```

---

### Design Rationale

**Structure:** The plan is organized with the Critical Path at the top (CP-1 through CP-7) covering the entire install-to-uninstall lifecycle including one complete workflow (QS). This is the minimum a tester must complete and takes roughly 2 hours. Remaining sections are grouped by area (CLI, Workflow Runtime, Edge Cases, Output Quality, Cross-Platform, Documentation, Regression) so a tester can work on one domain at a time.

**Why these specific critical path scenarios:** They trace the exact path every new user follows: `npx install` from registry, first agent activation and tier detection, first skill generation (QS is fastest), status check, update that preserves state, and uninstall. If any of these fail, the product cannot ship.

**Workflow coverage strategy:** Section 2 gives deep attention to the full quality path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX in scenario 2.4) and pipeline mode (2.5, 2.6) since those are the primary value flows. The remaining 10 workflows (AN, AS, US, SS, VS, RA, RS, DS, and batch/maintain pipeline) get smoke tests (2.12-2.22). This is realistic for a one-day testing window.

**What is deliberately excluded:** All behaviors already covered by automated tests (schema validation, file reference validation, Python script unit contracts, skill structural rules, workflow state consistency, markdown linting). The plan focuses purely on integration, runtime behavior, and subjective quality that only a human with an AI agent can evaluate.

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js` - Core installation logic tested by CP-1, CP-6, Section 1
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` - Agent activation, pipeline mode, and all workflow dispatch logic tested by CP-2, Section 2
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-quick-skill/SKILL.md` - Quick Skill workflow tested by CP-4, Section 2
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/platform-codes.yaml` - IDE installation targets tested by Section 1 and Section 5
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/references/pipeline-contracts.md` - Pipeline aliases and data flow tested by Section 2.5, 2.6, 2.22

input.content:
# Plan: Create Manual Test Plan for SKF v1.0.0 Release

## Context

All automated tests pass (137 Python + all JS suites, linting, formatting, 201 file refs). We just fixed 44 issues across 7 commits. Before shipping v1.0.0, we need a structured manual test plan covering behaviors that automated tests **cannot** validate: real npm registry execution, AI agent workflow runtime, cross-platform behavior, output quality, and documentation accuracy.

## What automated tests already cover (will NOT duplicate)

- Schema validation (52 fixtures), skill structure (14 rules), file references (201 refs)
- Installation component integrity (file existence, step chains)
- Knowledge base integrity (15 fragments, CSV index)
- Python script contracts (9 scripts, 137 tests)
- CLI integration in temp directories (install/update/uninstall)
- Formatting, linting, markdown quality

## File to create

**Path:** `docs/manual-test-plan.md`

**Estimated time for tester:** Critical Path ~2 hours | Full ~8 hours

### Structure (7 sections + critical path)

1. **Prerequisites & Environment Record** — Tool versions, OS, IDE, date
2. **Critical Path (CP-1 to CP-7)** — Minimum viable validation every tester must complete:
   - CP-1: Fresh install from npm registry
   - CP-2: Agent activation and first-run greeting
   - CP-3: Setup Forge (SF) tier detection
   - CP-4: Quick Skill (QS) end-to-end
   - CP-5: Status command accuracy
   - CP-6: Update preserving config/sidecar
   - CP-7: Uninstall completeness
3. **Section 1: CLI & Installer Extended** (11 scenarios) — Multi-IDE, auto-detection, reinstall, version check, cancel handling, learning opt-out, edge cases
4. **Section 2: Workflow Runtime** (22 scenarios) — All 14 workflows smoke-tested, pipeline mode (forge/forge-quick/maintain aliases), headless mode, batch mode, health check, KI/WS management commands
5. **Section 3: Edge Cases** (9 scenarios) — Missing config/sidecar, corrupt YAML, remote repos, docs-only source, version pinning, large repo performance, Python script invocation
6. **Section 4: Output Quality** (6 scenarios) — SKILL.md structure, context-snippet, metadata.json schema, provenance-map citation accuracy, export manifest, version directory structure
7. **Section 5: Cross-Platform** (5 scenarios) — macOS, Linux, Windows, Node version boundary, Python version boundary
8. **Section 6: Documentation** (5 scenarios) — Getting started walkthrough, workflows reference, concepts page, learning material, docs site vs actual behavior
9. **Section 7: Regression & Misc** (6 scenarios) — Legacy cleanup, compact greeting, communication language, custom folders, tool availability changes, git user resolution
10. **Results Summary Table + Issues Log + Sign-Off**

### Format

Each scenario has:
- Clear steps to execute
- Checkbox expectations (`- [ ]` for pass/fail tracking)
- Prerequisites listed per section

### Key design decisions

- Critical Path covers the exact new-user journey (install -> activate -> setup -> first skill -> status -> update -> uninstall)
- Full quality path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX) gets deep coverage in Section 2.4 (4 sub-scenarios)
- Remaining 10 workflows get smoke tests (enough to catch hard failures, not exhaustive)
- Pipeline mode tested with 3 aliases (forge, forge-quick, maintain)
- Output quality checks are manual spot-checks (citation accuracy, no hallucinated functions)

## Verification

After creating the file:
1. Run `npm run lint:md` to verify markdown quality
2. Count total scenarios (should be ~70+)
3. Verify the file is excluded from npm package (docs/ ships for learning material but that's intentional)

8. **Section 6: Documentation** (5 scenarios) — Getting started walkthrough, workflows reference, concepts page, learning material, docs site vs actual behavior
9. **Section 7: Regression & Misc** (6 scenarios) — Legacy cleanup, compact greeting, communication language, custom folders, tool availability changes, git user resolution
10. **Results Summary Table + Issues Log + Sign-Off**

### Format

Each scenario has:
- Clear steps to execute
- Checkbox expectations (`- [ ]` for pass/fail tracking)
- Prerequisites listed per section

### Key design decisions

- Critical Path covers the exact new-user journey (install -> activate -> setup -> first skill -> status -> update -> uninstall)
- Full quality path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX) gets deep coverage in Section 2.4 (4 sub-scenarios)
- Remaining 10 workflows get smoke tests (enough to catch hard failures, not exhaustive)
- Pipeline mode tested with 3 aliases (forge, forge-quick, maintain)
- Output quality checks are manual spot-checks (citation accuracy, no hallucinated functions)

## Verification

After creating the file:
1. Run `npm run lint:md` to verify markdown quality
2. Count total scenarios (should be ~70+)
3. Verify the file is excluded from npm package (docs/ ships for learning material but that's intentional)

# Plan: Create Manual Test Plan for SKF v1.0.0 Release

## Context

All automated tests pass (137 Python + all JS suites, linting, formatting, 201 file refs). We just fixed 44 issues across 7 commits. Before shipping v1.0.0, we need a structured manual test plan covering behaviors that automated tests **cannot** validate: real npm registry execution, AI agent workflow runtime, cross-platform behavior, output quality, and documentation accuracy.

## What automated tests already cover (will NOT duplicate)

- Schema validation (52 fixtures), skill structure (14 rules), file references (201 refs)
- Installation component integrity (file existence, step chains)
- Knowledge base integrity (15 fragments, CSV index)
- Python script contracts (9 scripts, 137 tests)
- CLI integration in temp directories (install/update/uninstall)
- Formatting, linting, markdown quality

## File to create

**Path:** `docs/manual-test-plan.md`

**Estimated time for tester:** Critical Path ~2 hours | Full ~8 hours

### Structure (7 sections + critical path)

1. **Prerequisites & Environment Record** — Tool versions, OS, IDE, date
2. **Critical Path (CP-1 to CP-7)** — Minimum viable validation every tester must complete:
   - CP-1: Fresh install from npm registry
   - CP-2: Agent activation and first-run greeting
   - CP-3: Setup Forge (SF) tier detection
   - CP-4: Quick Skill (QS) end-to-end
   - CP-5: Status command accuracy
   - CP-6: Update preserving config/sidecar
   - CP-7: Uninstall completeness
3. **Section 1: CLI & Installer Extended** (11 scenarios) — Multi-IDE, auto-detection, reinstall, version check, cancel handling, learning opt-out, edge cases
4. **Section 2: Workflow Runtime** (22 scenarios) — All 14 workflows smoke-tested, pipeline mode (forge/forge-quick/maintain aliases), headless mode, batch mode, health check, KI/WS management commands
5. **Section 3: Edge Cases** (9 scenarios) — Missing config/sidecar, corrupt YAML, remote repos, docs-only source, version pinning, large repo performance, Python script invocation
6. **Section 4: Output Quality** (6 scenarios) — SKILL.md structure, context-snippet, metadata.json schema, provenance-map citation accuracy, export manifest, version directory structure
7. **Section 5: Cross-Platform** (5 scenarios) — macOS, Linux, Windows, Node version boundary, Python version boundary
8. **Section 6: Documentation** (5 scenarios) — Getting started walkthrough, workflows reference, concepts page, learning material, docs site vs actual behavior
9. **Section 7: Regression & Misc** (6 scenarios) — Legacy cleanup, compact greeting, communication language, custom folders, tool availability changes, git user resolution
10. **Results Summary Table + Issues Log + Sign-Off**

### Format

Each scenario has:
- Clear steps to execute
- Checkbox expectations (`- [ ]` for pass/fail tracking)
- Prerequisites listed per section

### Key design decisions

- Critical Path covers the exact new-user journey (install -> activate -> setup -> first skill -> status -> update -> uninstall)
- Full quality path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX) gets deep coverage in Section 2.4 (4 sub-scenarios)
- Remaining 10 workflows get smoke tests (enough to catch hard failures, not exhaustive)
- Pipeline mode tested with 3 aliases (forge, forge-quick, maintain)
- Output quality checks are manual spot-checks (citation accuracy, no hallucinated functions)

## Verification

After creating the file:
1. Run `npm run lint:md` to verify markdown quality
2. Count total scenarios (should be ~70+)
3. Verify the file is excluded from npm package (docs/ ships for learning material but that's intentional)

input.content:
# SKF v1.0.0 Manual Test Plan

> **Purpose:** Validate behaviors that automated tests cannot cover —
> real npm registry execution, AI agent workflow runtime, cross-platform behavior,
> output quality, and documentation accuracy.
>
> **Estimated time:** Critical Path ~2 hours | Full Validation ~8 hours
>
> **Legend:**
>
> - `[ ]` = Not tested
> - `[x]` = Pass
> - `[!]` = Fail (add notes below scenario)
> - `[~]` = Blocked / Skipped (add reason)

## Prerequisites

Before starting any section, confirm:

- [ ] Node.js >= 22 installed (`node --version`)
- [ ] Python >= 3.10 installed (`python3 --version`)
- [ ] uv installed (`uv --version`)
- [ ] A clean test directory available (not the SKF repo itself)
- [ ] Internet access for npm registry and GitHub
- [ ] An AI IDE with agent skill support available (Claude Code recommended)
- [ ] `ast-grep` installed (for Forge/Forge+ tier testing)
- [ ] `gh` CLI installed and authenticated (for source access)
- [ ] (Optional) `ccc` (cocoindex-code) for Forge+ testing
- [ ] (Optional) `qmd` for Deep tier testing

Record your environment:

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| OS | |
| Node version | |
| Python version | |
| uv version | |
| AI IDE + version | |
| AI model used | |
| ast-grep version | |
| gh version | |
| ccc available? | |
| qmd available? | |
| Date tested | |
| SKF version tested | |

---

## Critical Path (Minimum Viable Validation)

> Complete these scenarios first. They trace the exact path every new user follows.
> Target: ~2 hours.

### CP-1: Fresh Install from npm Registry

**Prerequisites:** Empty test directory, no prior SKF installation.

**Steps:**

1. `cd` into the empty test directory
2. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
3. Enter a project name (e.g., "test-project")
4. Accept default output folders ("skills", "forge-data")
5. Select your AI IDE (e.g., "Claude Code")
6. Accept learning material installation

**Expected:**

- [ ] npx downloads the package from the npm registry (not a local copy)
- [ ] Banner displays with "SKF" ASCII art and version number
- [ ] All prompts render correctly with styled terminal output (no ANSI artifacts)
- [ ] Installation completes with spinner progress for each step
- [ ] `_bmad/skf/` created with: module.yaml, config.yaml, VERSION, all `skf-*` skill directories, knowledge/, shared/
- [ ] `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/` created with forge-tier.yaml and preferences.yaml
- [ ] `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml` created with version, file lists, IDE list
- [ ] `.gitignore` updated with `_bmad/_memory/` entry
- [ ] IDE skill directories created (e.g., `.claude/skills/`) with all skill content
- [ ] `skills/` and `forge-data/` directories created with `.gitkeep` files
- [ ] `_skf-learn/` directory created with documentation content
- [ ] Success message displays "Get Started" instructions referencing `@Ferris SF`
- [ ] config.yaml contains correct project_name, ides list, and folder paths

### CP-2: Agent Activation and First-Run Greeting

**Prerequisites:** CP-1 completed. AI IDE open in the test directory.

**Steps:**

1. Open the project in your AI IDE
2. Invoke Ferris: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Ferris loads config.yaml and resolves all variables
- [ ] Ferris detects first-run state (forge-tier.yaml has `tier: ~`)
- [ ] Greeting addresses you by name (from git config or "Developer")
- [ ] Ferris dispatches to the Setup Forge workflow

### CP-3: Setup Forge (SF) — Tier Detection

**Prerequisites:** CP-2 in progress.

**Steps:**

1. Let the SF workflow run to completion

**Expected:**

- [ ] Each tool is detected (ast-grep, gh, ccc, qmd) — results shown
- [ ] Tier assigned correctly based on detected tools
- [ ] `forge-tier.yaml` written with correct tool availability and tier
- [ ] `preferences.yaml` exists with defaults (headless_mode: false)
- [ ] Status report displays the detected tier, tools, and configuration
- [ ] Health check runs — "Clean run. No workflow issues to report."

### CP-4: Quick Skill (QS) — End-to-End

**Prerequisites:** CP-3 completed. Fresh agent session recommended.

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris QS chalk` (or another small, well-known package)
2. Let the workflow proceed through all steps
3. When the review gate appears, review the compiled output and continue

**Expected:**

- [ ] Step 1: Repository resolved from package name
- [ ] Step 2: Ecosystem check — reports findings or proceeds
- [ ] Step 3: Extracts public API surface from the resolved source
- [ ] Step 4: Compiled SKILL.md presented for review
- [ ] Step 5: Validates the compiled output (runs Python validation script)
- [ ] Step 6: Files written to `skills/{skill-name}/v{version}/`
- [ ] SKILL.md exists with: frontmatter, Overview, API sections, citations
- [ ] context-snippet.md exists
- [ ] metadata.json exists with name, version, source info, confidence tier
- [ ] Health check runs at workflow completion

### CP-5: Status Command

**Prerequisites:** CP-4 completed.

**Steps:**

1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge status`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Shows "Skill Forge — Status" with version
- [ ] Installation section: project name, SKF folder, agent status, skill count
- [ ] IDEs section: lists configured IDE(s) with green indicators
- [ ] Forge Tier section: tier name, detection date, tool availability
- [ ] Output Folders section: skills/ and forge-data/ with existence checkmarks
- [ ] Sidecar section: initialized state and path

### CP-6: Update Preserving Config

**Prerequisites:** CP-4 completed.

**Steps:**

1. Edit `_bmad/skf/config.yaml` — change `project_name` to "custom-name-test"
2. Note the current forge-tier.yaml contents
3. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge update`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Update message displays "Quick Update" with "preserving config and sidecar"
- [ ] SKF files replaced (VERSION updated, workflow files refreshed)
- [ ] config.yaml still contains "custom-name-test"
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml unchanged (tier and tool detections preserved)
- [ ] preferences.yaml unchanged
- [ ] IDE skill directories refreshed
- [ ] Generated skills in `skills/` are untouched

### CP-7: Uninstall Completeness

**Prerequisites:** CP-6 completed.

**Steps:**

1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge uninstall`
2. Review the removal plan displayed
3. Confirm when prompted

**Expected:**

- [ ] Removal plan shows categories with file counts
- [ ] After confirmation: IDE skill directories cleaned
- [ ] `_skf-learn/` removed
- [ ] Sidecar directory removed
- [ ] SKF module directory removed
- [ ] Manifest file removed
- [ ] Generated skills with actual content remain untouched
- [ ] "SKF uninstalled successfully" displayed

---

## Section 1: CLI and Installer (Extended)

### 1.1: Multi-IDE Installation

**Steps:**

1. Fresh directory. Run install, select 3+ IDEs (e.g., Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Skill directories created for each selected IDE
- [ ] Each IDE directory contains all skill content
- [ ] config.yaml `ides` field lists all selected IDE codes

### 1.2: "Other" Only IDE Selection

**Steps:**

1. Fresh directory. Run install, select only "Other"

**Expected:**

- [ ] No IDE-specific skill directories created
- [ ] config.yaml `ides` contains `['other']`
- [ ] Installation succeeds without errors

### 1.3: IDE Auto-Detection

**Prerequisites:** Test directory with a pre-existing `.claude/` directory.

**Steps:**

1. Run install, observe the IDE selection prompt

**Expected:**

- [ ] Log message shows "Auto-detected IDEs: claude-code"
- [ ] Claude Code is pre-selected in the multiselect

### 1.4: Reinstall (Fresh) Over Existing

**Steps:**

1. On an existing installation, run install
2. Select "Fresh install — Remove everything and start over"
3. Complete with different settings

**Expected:**

- [ ] Old installation removed before new one created
- [ ] New config.yaml reflects new settings
- [ ] Previous config defaults pre-populated in prompts

### 1.5: Version Check Notification

**Steps:**

1. If testing with a pre-release: run any CLI command, observe output after completion

**Expected:**

- [ ] If newer version exists: update notice appears after command output
- [ ] Notice shows current and latest versions with upgrade command
- [ ] If no update: no notice (silent)

### 1.6: npx Without Subcommand

**Steps:**

1. Run `npx bmad-module-skill-forge` (no subcommand)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Help output displays with available commands
- [ ] Version shown

### 1.7: Install Into Existing \_bmad Directory (Non-SKF)

**Prerequisites:** Directory with `_bmad/` but no `_bmad/skf/`.

**Expected:**

- [ ] `_bmad/skf/` created alongside existing contents
- [ ] No "existing installation" prompt

### 1.8: Uninstall Without Manifest

**Prerequisites:** Delete `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml` manually.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Warning: asks to reinstall first to generate manifest
- [ ] No files deleted

### 1.9: Cancel During Installation

**Steps:**

1. Start install, press Ctrl+C at any prompt

**Expected:**

- [ ] "Installation cancelled" displayed
- [ ] No partial files left behind

### 1.10: Learning Material Opt-Out

**Steps:**

1. During install, decline learning material

**Expected:**

- [ ] No `_skf-learn/` directory created
- [ ] config.yaml has `install_learning: false`

### 1.11: Custom Output Folder Names

**Steps:**

1. During install, enter custom names (e.g., "my-skills", "my-forge-data")

**Expected:**

- [ ] Custom directories created with `.gitkeep`
- [ ] config.yaml reflects custom paths
- [ ] Workflows use the custom paths for output

---

## Section 2: Workflow Runtime (Agent-Driven)

> Start a fresh agent session for each workflow to prevent context contamination.

### 2.1: Setup Forge — Quick Tier (No Tools)

**Prerequisites:** ast-grep not available.

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Tier detected as "Quick"
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml shows tool availability as null/false
- [ ] Report acknowledges Quick tier positively

### 2.2: Setup Forge — Forge Tier (ast-grep Only)

**Prerequisites:** ast-grep available, no ccc/qmd.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Tier detected as "Forge"
- [ ] forge-tier.yaml shows ast_grep: available

### 2.3: Setup Forge — Tier Override

**Steps:**

1. Edit preferences.yaml: set `tier_override: Quick`
2. Fresh session: `@Ferris SF`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Report acknowledges the tier override
- [ ] Workflow behaves at Quick tier regardless of available tools

### 2.4: Full Quality Path (BS -> CS -> TS -> EX)

> This is the most important workflow test. Run each in a separate fresh session.

#### 2.4a: Brief Skill (BS)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris BS`
2. Provide target repo URL, skill name, scope hints
3. Confirm the brief

**Expected:**

- [ ] Guided discovery through scope definition
- [ ] skill-brief.yaml written to forge-data/
- [ ] Brief contains: name, version, source_repo, scope, language

#### 2.4b: Create Skill (CS)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris CS`
2. Provide path to skill-brief.yaml from 2.4a

**Expected:**

- [ ] Brief loaded successfully
- [ ] Source extracted with citations
- [ ] SKILL.md compiled with frontmatter, API sections, provenance citations
- [ ] All artifacts written: SKILL.md, context-snippet.md, metadata.json
- [ ] provenance-map.json exists (if Forge tier or above)

#### 2.4c: Test Skill (TS)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris TS`
2. Provide skill name from 2.4b

**Expected:**

- [ ] Skill loaded and analyzed
- [ ] Coverage check reports export coverage percentage
- [ ] Score computed with breakdown by category
- [ ] Report with PASS/FAIL verdict written to forge-data/
- [ ] compute-score.py invoked successfully

#### 2.4d: Export Skill (EX)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris EX`
2. Provide skill name, confirm context file updates

**Expected:**

- [ ] .export-manifest.json created/updated
- [ ] Context snippet injection into platform context file (CLAUDE.md etc.)
- [ ] Managed section has clear markers
- [ ] Export summary with token cost estimate

### 2.5: Pipeline Mode — forge Alias

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris forge chalk` (or another target)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Pipeline parsed: BS -> CS -> TS -> EX
- [ ] Headless mode activated automatically
- [ ] Progress: "Pipeline [1/4]: Starting BS..."
- [ ] Data forwarded between workflows
- [ ] Pipeline summary at end

### 2.6: Pipeline Mode — forge-quick Alias

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris forge-quick <package-name>`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Pipeline parsed: QS -> TS -> EX
- [ ] Completes or halts at circuit breaker

### 2.7: Headless Mode — Flag

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris QS <package-name> --headless`

**Expected:**

- [ ] All gates auto-resolve with default action
- [ ] Progress output still displays
- [ ] Output written to disk without interaction

### 2.8: Headless Mode — Preferences

**Steps:**

1. Set `headless_mode: true` in preferences.yaml
2. Fresh session: `@Ferris QS <package-name>` (no -H flag)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Headless mode active from preferences
- [ ] Same behavior as 2.7

### 2.9: Health Check — Clean Run

After any successful workflow:

- [ ] "Health Check: Clean run. No workflow issues to report."
- [ ] No fabricated issues

### 2.10: Knowledge Index (KI)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session: `@Ferris KI`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Displays 15 knowledge fragments from index
- [ ] Shows id, name, description, tags, tier for each

### 2.11: Workflow Status (WS)

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session (after some workflows): `@Ferris WS`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Shows current forge tier and tool availability
- [ ] Shows active skill briefs (if any)

### 2.12: Analyze Source (AN) — Smoke Test

**Steps:**

1. Fresh session in a project with actual source code: `@Ferris AN`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Scans project and identifies skillable units
- [ ] Presents recommendations
- [ ] Generates skill-brief.yaml files

### 2.13: Audit Skill (AS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A previously generated skill with accessible source.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Re-indexes source for comparison
- [ ] Produces drift report with severity classification

### 2.14: Update Skill (US) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A skill where source has changed.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Detects changes and re-extracts
- [ ] Preserves [MANUAL] sections
- [ ] Updated artifacts written

### 2.15: Stack Skill (SS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** A project with dependency manifest (package.json etc.).

**Expected:**

- [ ] Detects dependencies from manifest
- [ ] Ranks by significance
- [ ] Compiles consolidated stack skill with integration patterns
- [ ] metadata.json has skill_type: "stack"

### 2.16: Verify Stack (VS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** Architecture doc and generated skills.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Cross-references skills against architecture
- [ ] Produces feasibility report citing specific APIs

### 2.17: Refine Architecture (RA) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** Architecture doc and generated skills.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Gap analysis performed
- [ ] Improvements suggested with API evidence

### 2.18: Rename Skill (RS) — Smoke Test

**Prerequisites:** An existing generated skill.

**Expected:**

- [ ] New name validated (no collision)
- [ ] Copy-before-delete: new materialized before old removed
- [ ] All references updated (manifest, metadata)

### 2.19: Drop Skill (DS) — Soft Deprecation

**Expected:**

- [ ] Confirmation required
- [ ] Manifest updated with deprecation status
- [ ] Files retained on disk

### 2.20: Drop Skill (DS) — Hard Purge

**Expected:**

- [ ] Confirmation with irreversibility warning
- [ ] Files deleted from disk
- [ ] Manifest updated

### 2.21: Batch Mode (CS)

**Prerequisites:** Multiple skill-brief.yaml files in forge-data/.

**Expected:**

- [ ] All briefs discovered and processed sequentially
- [ ] Separate output directories for each skill
- [ ] Summary of all compiled skills

### 2.22: Pipeline — maintain Alias

**Prerequisites:** Existing skill with source changes.

**Expected:**

- [ ] Pipeline parsed: AS -> US -> TS -> EX
- [ ] If AS finds CLEAN drift — US skipped
- [ ] If AS finds drift — US proceeds

---

## Section 3: Edge Cases

### 3.1: Missing Sidecar Directory

**Steps:**

1. Delete `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/`, invoke Ferris

**Expected:**

- [ ] Meaningful guidance (not a crash)
- [ ] Suggests running SF to reinitialize

### 3.2: Missing Config File

**Steps:**

1. Delete `_bmad/skf/config.yaml`, invoke Ferris

**Expected:**

- [ ] HARD HALT: clear error message about missing config
- [ ] Actionable guidance (reinstall or create config)

### 3.3: Corrupt Config (Invalid YAML)

**Steps:**

1. Replace config.yaml with `:::bad yaml:::`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Meaningful error (not raw YAML parse stack trace)
- [ ] Actionable guidance

### 3.4: Remote Repository Source

**Steps:**

1. `@Ferris QS https://github.com/lodash/lodash`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Source resolved from GitHub URL
- [ ] Skill generated with correct source_repo reference

### 3.5: Package Name Resolution

**Steps:**

1. `@Ferris QS express` (resolves via npm registry)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Package name resolved to GitHub repository
- [ ] Correct repository used for extraction

### 3.6: Docs-Only Source Type

**Steps:**

1. `@Ferris BS` with a target that has no accessible source code (docs URL only)

**Expected:**

- [ ] docs-only mode activated
- [ ] All citations use [EXT:...] format
- [ ] Scoring applies docs-only weight redistribution

### 3.7: Version-Pinned Skill

**Steps:**

1. `@Ferris QS chalk@5.0.0`

**Expected:**

- [ ] Version extracted from invocation
- [ ] Output at `skills/chalk/v5.0.0/`
- [ ] metadata.json records the pinned version

### 3.8: Python Script Invocation

**Steps:**

1. Run TS workflow (triggers compute-score.py)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Python script invoked successfully
- [ ] Score returned as valid JSON
- [ ] No import errors or missing dependencies

### 3.9: Large Repository Performance

**Steps:**

1. `@Ferris QS https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript` (or similar large repo)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Workflow does not hang or time out
- [ ] Extraction scopes appropriately
- [ ] Result produced within reasonable time
- [ ] Note any performance issues: ________________________________________________

---

## Section 4: Output Quality

> Perform these checks on at least one generated skill.

### 4.1: SKILL.md Structure and Content

- [ ] Valid YAML frontmatter with name, description
- [ ] Overview section present
- [ ] API/function sections with signatures (parameter names, types, return types)
- [ ] Source citations in format `[AST:path/file.py:L42]` or `[SRC:path:L42]`
- [ ] No hallucinated content (functions that don't exist in source)
- [ ] Clean markdown formatting

### 4.2: context-snippet.md Quality

- [ ] Concise summary (not a copy of SKILL.md)
- [ ] Contains key API entry points
- [ ] Suitable for context injection
- [ ] Clean markdown

### 4.3: metadata.json Schema

- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Contains: name, version, source_repo, source_type
- [ ] Confidence tier information present
- [ ] Timestamps present

### 4.4: provenance-map.json Citation Accuracy

**Prerequisites:** Skill generated at Forge tier or above.

- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Each entry maps instruction to source location
- [ ] Source paths are relative and valid
- [ ] Spot-check 3-5 citations against actual source files
- [ ] Confidence tiers assigned (T1, T1-low, T2, T3)

### 4.5: Export Manifest

Check `skills/{name}/.export-manifest.json`:

- [ ] Valid JSON
- [ ] Lists exported versions
- [ ] Active version correctly identified

### 4.6: Version Directory Structure

- [ ] Skills stored at `skills/{name}/v{version}/`
- [ ] Active symlink or reference points to correct version
- [ ] (Optional) Generate two versions — verify both coexist

---

## Section 5: Cross-Platform

> Run the Critical Path (or at minimum CP-1 through CP-5) on each target platform.

### 5.1: macOS

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- Notes: ________________________________________________

### 5.2: Linux

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] Status command works
- Notes: ________________________________________________

### 5.3: Windows

- [ ] Fresh install succeeds
- [ ] Paths use correct separators
- [ ] Tier detection works
- [ ] QS workflow completes
- [ ] .gitignore uses forward slashes (cross-platform)
- Known caveats: Symlinks may require admin privileges
- Notes: ________________________________________________

### 5.4: Node.js Version Boundary

- [ ] Node.js 22.x: all CLI commands work
- [ ] Node.js < 22: engines check blocks or warns

### 5.5: Python Version Boundary

- [ ] Python 3.10: all shared scripts work
- [ ] Python < 3.10: meaningful error from scripts

---

## Section 6: Documentation

### 6.1: Getting Started Guide Walkthrough

Follow docs/getting-started.md step by step on a fresh project:

- [ ] Installation command works as documented
- [ ] Prerequisites table is accurate
- [ ] "First Steps" instructions invoke correctly
- [ ] Links work

### 6.2: Workflows Reference Accuracy

Check docs/workflows.md:

- [ ] All 14 workflows listed with correct trigger codes
- [ ] Pipeline aliases documented correctly
- [ ] Headless mode behavior described

### 6.3: Concepts Page Accuracy

Check docs/concepts.md:

- [ ] Key terms defined correctly (forge tier, confidence tier, sidecar, etc.)
- [ ] No stale references

### 6.4: Learning Material Completeness

Check `_skf-learn/` after installation:

- [ ] Contains docs content
- [ ] Files are readable and self-contained

### 6.5: Docs Site vs Actual Behavior

- [ ] IDE count matches (23 IDEs)
- [ ] Tier descriptions match detection logic
- [ ] Pipeline alias expansions match actual behavior

---

## Section 7: Regression and Misc

### 7.1: Returning User Compact Greeting

**Steps:**

1. Set `compact_greeting: true` in preferences.yaml
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**

- [ ] Brief greeting (no full capabilities table)
- [ ] Full table shown only if requested

### 7.2: Communication Language

**Steps:**

1. Set `communication_language: fr` in config.yaml
2. Fresh session: invoke Ferris

**Expected:**

- [ ] Ferris communicates in the configured language

### 7.3: Concurrent Tool Availability Changes

**Steps:**

1. Run SF, note tier
2. Install or remove a tool (e.g., ast-grep)
3. Run SF again

**Expected:**

- [ ] New tool state detected on re-run
- [ ] Tier updated accordingly

### 7.4: Git Config User Name Resolution

- [ ] user_name in config.yaml matches `git config user.name`
- [ ] If git config unset, falls back to "Developer"

### 7.5: Pipeline Anti-Pattern Detection

**Steps:**

1. Try `@Ferris EX CS TS` (export before create)

**Expected:**

- [ ] Warning or error about invalid pipeline order

### 7.6: Circuit Breaker Halt

**Steps:**

1. Run a pipeline where TS score falls below threshold

**Expected:**

- [ ] Pipeline halts before next workflow
- [ ] Summary shows completed/remaining workflows
